name: Business Valuation
subtitle: Level 5 · Owner · Financials & Metrics
whatThisIs:
Business valuation is not about preparing to sell tomorrow. It is about understanding what drives the value of your business today so you can engineer that value over time. When you know what buyers look for, every strategic decision becomes a valuation decision.

Most owners discover their business value at the point of sale — and that is too late. By then, the deal structure, timing, and price are largely fixed by choices you made years ago. This playbook changes that. You start thinking like a buyer while you still have time to build what they want to pay for.

This covers the fundamentals of how businesses are valued, what drives those valuations higher, and the specific areas you can work on to increase enterprise value. Not exit planning — that sits in the 5.2 Exit Strategy Playbook. This is about value creation.

broken.label: When this is broken
broken.content: You have no idea what your business is worth or what drives that value. Revenue feels like the only thing that matters. You make strategic decisions based on short-term cash needs instead of long-term value creation. The thought of being valued makes you nervous because you suspect it would be disappointing. You run the business for lifestyle, not enterprise value.

ok.label: When this is OK
ok.content: You have a rough sense that your business might be worth a few times revenue, but you are not sure how that multiple gets decided. You know some things probably matter more than others for valuation but you could not list them. You make good business decisions but do not explicitly consider their impact on enterprise value. You suspect you could be doing more to build a valuable asset.

working.label: When this is working
working.content: You know what your business is worth using multiple valuation methods and track how that value changes over time. Every strategic decision includes a consideration of its impact on enterprise value. Your revenue is predictable, your client base is diversified, and your operations run without you. You have built something that buyers would compete for — whether you plan to sell it or not.

thingsToThinkAbout: How predictable is your revenue really? Buyers pay more for businesses they can forecast with confidence. One-off projects and lumpy sales create risk. Recurring contracts, retainers, and subscription elements create certainty. The more predictable your income, the higher the multiple buyers will apply to it.

Could the business operate without you for six months? Owner dependence kills valuations faster than anything else. If you are the main salesperson, key relationship holder, or decision maker for everything, you are not selling a business — you are selling a job. Transferability drives value.

Is your client base a strength or a risk? Twenty percent of revenue from your biggest client might feel great for cash flow, but it is a red flag for buyers. Concentration risk reduces valuations. Geographic, industry, and client diversification protects and increases value.

What do you actually own? Intellectual property, proprietary systems, brand recognition, exclusive partnerships — these are the assets that differentiate your business from competitors. If everything you do could be replicated easily, that limits what someone would pay to acquire it rather than build it themselves.

How does your business compare to others that have sold? Industry multiples exist for a reason. Knowing where your sector typically trades gives you a baseline, but the best businesses in any sector command premium multiples. What would put you in that premium category?
quickWins: This week: Calculate your business value using the asset, income, and market approaches. The exact number matters less than understanding how each method works and what drives the differences.
This week: List your top 10 clients and calculate what percentage of revenue each represents. If any single client is above 15% of total revenue, that is your first risk to address.
This week: Write down all the intellectual property, systems, or unique advantages your business has that competitors could not easily replicate. If the list is short, that tells you where to focus.

actionSections.title: When you open this playbook in the Profit System, you will find:

actions.title: Valuation Methods & Benchmarking
actions.description: Learn the three main ways businesses are valued and apply them to get a baseline for your business. Compare your metrics against industry standards and recently sold businesses to understand where you sit in the market.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Learn the three main methods for valuing a small business, apply each one to your own business, and compare your metrics against industry standards and recently sold businesses. This gives you a realistic picture of what your business is worth today, and a baseline to improve from.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: Most business owners have no idea what their business is worth. Some massively overestimate based on revenue. Others massively underestimate because they have never thought about it. Knowing your valuation matters whether or not you plan to sell. It tells you whether you are building an asset or just running a job. It reveals which financial levers matter most. And if you ever do want to sell, retire, or bring in a partner, you need a number that is grounded in reality, not wishful thinking.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through each valuation method, calculate a range, and then benchmark against comparable businesses in your industry.

---

detailSections.title: Method 1: Earnings Multiple (Most Common for Small Businesses)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Calculate your Seller's Discretionary Earnings (SDE)** - Start with net profit, then add back: owner's salary, owner's benefits, one-off expenses, depreciation, and interest. SDE represents the true economic benefit to an owner-operator.
- [ ] **Identify the typical earnings multiple for your industry** - Most small service businesses sell for 2-4x SDE. Product businesses may be higher. Look up benchmarks on BizBuySell, the BVR (Business Valuation Resources), or ask a business broker for recent comparables.
- [ ] **Multiply your SDE by the multiple range** - This gives you a valuation range. Example: SDE of £150,000 at a 2.5-3.5x multiple = £375,000 to £525,000.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: If you do not know your SDE, start with last year's net profit plus your total salary and benefits. That rough number is close enough for a first estimate.

detailSections.title: Method 2: Revenue Multiple
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Take your annual revenue** - Use the last 12 months or an average of the last 3 years if revenue has been volatile.
- [ ] **Identify the typical revenue multiple for your industry** - Most small businesses sell for 0.5-1.5x revenue. High-growth, high-margin, or recurring-revenue businesses command higher multiples.
- [ ] **Multiply revenue by the multiple range** - This gives a second valuation estimate. Revenue multiples are less precise than earnings multiples but useful as a cross-check.

detailSections.title: Method 3: Asset-Based Valuation
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List all tangible business assets** - Equipment, inventory, property, vehicles, cash reserves, accounts receivable.
- [ ] **List intangible assets** - Customer list, brand reputation, intellectual property, proprietary systems, contracts, trained team.
- [ ] **Subtract all liabilities** - Loans, debts, accounts payable, lease obligations, pending legal issues.
- [ ] **Calculate net asset value** - Total assets minus total liabilities. This is the floor value of your business. If your earnings-based valuation is lower than your asset value, something is wrong with profitability.

detailSections.title: Benchmark Against Comparables
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Research 3-5 recently sold businesses in your industry and size range** - BizBuySell, business broker reports, industry association data, or direct conversations with brokers. Note their revenue, profit, and sale price.
- [ ] **Compare your key metrics** - Revenue growth rate, profit margin, customer concentration, recurring revenue percentage. Where do you sit relative to businesses that sold well?
- [ ] **Identify the gaps** - Which metrics are pulling your valuation down? These become your improvement priorities in the other actions in this playbook.
- [ ] **Document your valuation range** - Write down all three method results and your best estimate of current market value. This is your baseline.
- [ ] **Set a date to reassess in 12 months** - Track whether your actions are increasing business value over time.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- Three independent valuation estimates using different methods
- A realistic valuation range based on real data, not guesswork
- Benchmark comparisons against similar businesses that have sold
- Clear gaps identified between current valuation and potential
- A documented baseline to measure improvement against

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
The first valuation is often a shock, in both directions. Some clients discover their business is worth less than they hoped because profits are low despite strong revenue. Others discover hidden value they did not appreciate, like a loyal customer base or strong recurring revenue.

The most important number is SDE, not revenue. Many business owners focus on top-line revenue, but buyers care about earnings. A £1M revenue business with £50K profit is worth less than a £500K revenue business with £200K profit. Help clients shift their focus.

Do not let clients get discouraged by a low first valuation. The whole point is to create a baseline and then use the remaining actions in this playbook to increase it systematically. The number today is just the starting point.

actions.title: Revenue Predictability Audit
actions.description: Analyse how much of your revenue is recurring, contracted, or otherwise predictable versus one-off or project-based. Build a plan to increase the predictable portion and reduce revenue volatility.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Analyse how much of your revenue is recurring, contracted, or predictable versus one-off or unpredictable. Then build a plan to increase the predictable portion. Predictable revenue is the single biggest driver of business valuation because it reduces risk for any future buyer and gives you, the owner, more stability and confidence.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: A buyer will pay significantly more for a business where next month's revenue is already locked in than for a business where every month starts from zero. John Warrillow's "Built to Sell" framework makes this clear: recurring revenue businesses sell for 3-5x higher multiples than project-based businesses. But this is not just about selling. Predictable revenue changes how you run the business day to day. You can plan hiring, invest in growth, and sleep at night because you are not wondering whether next month will be a good month or a disaster.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Categorise all your revenue, calculate your predictability score, and identify specific moves to shift more revenue into predictable categories.

---

detailSections.title: Categorise Your Revenue
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Pull your last 12 months of revenue data** - Every invoice, payment, and transaction. Group by customer and by product/service.
- [ ] **Sort every revenue line into one of four categories:**
  - **Recurring** - Subscription, retainer, or membership. Customer pays regularly without a new sales conversation each time. (Score: 4)
  - **Contracted** - Customer has signed a contract for future work, even if not subscription-based. Committed but with an end date. (Score: 3)
  - **Repeat** - Customer buys from you regularly but has not committed to future purchases. They could stop at any time. (Score: 2)
  - **One-off** - Single transaction with no expectation of repeat. New customer each time. (Score: 1)
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: Add up the percentage of revenue in each category. Most small businesses are shocked to discover that 60-80% of their revenue is one-off or repeat, not recurring or contracted.

detailSections.title: Calculate Your Predictability Score
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Multiply each category's revenue by its score (1-4)** - Then divide the total by your overall revenue and multiply by 25 to get a percentage score.
- [ ] **Interpret your score:**
  - 80-100% = Highly predictable (strong valuation driver)
  - 60-79% = Moderately predictable (room for improvement)
  - 40-59% = Low predictability (significant valuation drag)
  - Below 40% = Unpredictable (biggest priority to fix)
- [ ] **Compare to your industry** - Some industries naturally have more recurring revenue (SaaS, coaching, maintenance contracts). Others are inherently project-based. Know what "good" looks like for your sector.

detailSections.title: Identify Shifts You Can Make
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Review your one-off revenue** - Can any of these customers be converted to a repeat or recurring relationship? What would you need to offer?
- [ ] **Review your repeat customers** - Can you offer them a contract, retainer, or subscription that locks in their spending and gives them a benefit (discount, priority, guaranteed capacity)?
- [ ] **Explore new recurring revenue models** - Maintenance plans, membership tiers, advisory retainers, subscription add-ons, annual contracts with monthly billing. What could work for your business?
- [ ] **Calculate the impact** - If you shifted 20% of your one-off revenue to recurring, what would your new predictability score be? What would that mean for valuation?
- [ ] **Pick one shift to implement this quarter** - Do not try to change everything. Move one revenue stream toward greater predictability and prove the concept.
- [ ] **Set a 6-month review to remeasure your predictability score** - Track the trend over time.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear picture of how your revenue breaks down across predictability categories
- A calculated predictability score with benchmark comparison
- Specific opportunities identified to shift revenue toward recurring or contracted
- One concrete action chosen to implement this quarter
- A baseline score to measure improvement against

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This audit often triggers a strategic rethink. Clients who have always sold projects start asking "how do I create a retainer?" Clients who have been undercharging start seeing the value of annual contracts. The insight alone is powerful.

The easiest shift is usually from repeat to contracted. If customers already buy from you regularly, a simple annual agreement with a small discount can lock in that revenue. Start there because it requires no new products, just a new structure.

Watch for clients who try to force recurring revenue where it does not fit. Not every business can be a subscription model. The goal is to increase predictability, not to pretend you are a SaaS company. Even shifting from 30% predictable to 50% predictable makes a meaningful difference to valuation.

actions.title: Value Driver Assessment
actions.description: Score your business across the key factors that drive valuation: financial performance, growth potential, market position, management team, systems, and scalability. Identify which drivers need strengthening.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Score your business across the key factors that drive valuation: financial performance, growth potential, market position, management team, systems and processes, and scalability. This assessment reveals exactly which areas are adding value and which are holding your valuation back, so you can focus improvement where it matters most.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: Business valuation is not just about profit. Two businesses with identical earnings can have wildly different valuations because of their underlying drivers. A business with strong systems, a management team that runs independently, and a growing market is worth multiples more than a business with the same profit but no systems, total owner dependency, and a shrinking market. Understanding your value drivers lets you build the business that is worth the most, not just the one that earns the most today.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Score yourself honestly on each value driver, identify your weakest areas, and create an improvement plan.

---

detailSections.title: Score Your Financial Performance (1-10)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Revenue growth trend** - Has revenue grown consistently over the last 3 years? (1 = declining, 5 = flat, 10 = strong consistent growth)
- [ ] **Profit margin** - Are your margins healthy for your industry? (1 = breakeven or loss, 5 = average, 10 = industry-leading)
- [ ] **Revenue predictability** - What percentage of revenue is recurring or contracted? (Use your score from the Revenue Predictability Audit)
- [ ] **Customer concentration** - Does any single customer represent more than 15% of revenue? (1 = one customer dominates, 10 = highly diversified)

detailSections.title: Score Your Growth Potential (1-10)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Market size and trajectory** - Is your market growing, stable, or shrinking? (1 = shrinking, 5 = stable, 10 = rapidly growing)
- [ ] **Product pipeline** - Do you have new products or improvements in development? (1 = nothing planned, 10 = strong pipeline with validated demand)
- [ ] **Expansion opportunities** - Could you enter new markets, geographies, or customer segments? (1 = maxed out, 10 = multiple untapped opportunities)
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: Check if your revenue has grown in each of the last 3 years. Consistent growth, even modest, scores much higher than volatile revenue.

detailSections.title: Score Your Market Position (1-10)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Competitive differentiation** - How clearly do customers understand why you are different? (1 = commodity, interchangeable with competitors, 10 = unique position that competitors cannot easily replicate)
- [ ] **Brand recognition** - Are you known in your market? Do customers seek you out? (1 = unknown, 10 = recognised authority)
- [ ] **Customer loyalty** - What is your retention rate and repeat purchase rate? (1 = high churn, 10 = customers stay for years and refer others)

detailSections.title: Score Your Management and Team (1-10)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Owner dependency** - Could the business operate for 4 weeks without you? (1 = would collapse immediately, 10 = runs smoothly without you)
- [ ] **Management depth** - Do you have capable people who can make decisions independently? (1 = you make every decision, 10 = strong management team in place)
- [ ] **Team retention** - Do good people stay? (1 = constant turnover, 10 = stable, loyal team)

detailSections.title: Score Your Systems and Scalability (1-10)
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Process documentation** - Are key processes documented so anyone could follow them? (1 = everything is in people's heads, 10 = fully documented and maintained)
- [ ] **Technology infrastructure** - Do your systems support growth or hold it back? (1 = manual and fragile, 10 = automated and scalable)
- [ ] **Scalability** - Could you double revenue without doubling costs? (1 = costs scale linearly with revenue, 10 = significant operating leverage)

detailSections.title: Calculate and Interpret
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Average your scores across all categories** - This is your overall Value Driver Score.
- [ ] **Identify your 3 lowest-scoring areas** - These are the constraints on your valuation. Fixing these will have the biggest impact.
- [ ] **Create an improvement plan** - For each of your 3 weakest areas, define one specific action to improve the score over the next 6 months.
- [ ] **Set a reassessment date in 6 months** - Track your progress and see which scores have improved.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A scored assessment across all major value drivers
- Clear visibility on which factors are strengthening and weakening your valuation
- Your 3 highest-priority improvement areas identified
- A 6-month improvement plan with specific actions
- A repeatable assessment you can use to track progress over time

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
Clients often score themselves too generously on the first pass. Push for honesty by asking concrete questions. "Can the business run for 4 weeks without you?" is more revealing than "how dependent is the business on you?" Use specific scenarios to calibrate scores.

The most common weak areas are owner dependency and process documentation. These are also the areas most within the owner's control to fix, which makes them good coaching targets. Revenue growth and market position take longer to change.

Remind clients that no business scores 10 across the board. The goal is not perfection. It is systematic improvement on the things that matter most for valuation. Even moving your weakest area from a 3 to a 6 can meaningfully increase what the business is worth.

actions.title: Transferability Review
actions.description: Audit how dependent your business is on you personally and create a roadmap to reduce that dependency. Cover sales, operations, strategy, and key relationships so the business can thrive without you.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Audit how dependent your business is on you personally across four areas: sales, operations, strategy, and relationships. Then create a roadmap to reduce that dependency so the business could operate, and eventually be sold, without you at the centre of everything.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: A business that cannot function without its owner is not really a business. It is a job with overhead. Michael Gerber made this point in The E-Myth decades ago, and it remains the number one reason small businesses sell for less than they should, or cannot sell at all. Every task, decision, relationship, and process that depends on you personally reduces your business's value. A buyer is not buying you. They are buying a machine that produces profit. If the machine breaks the moment you leave, it is not worth much.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through each dependency area, identify the critical risks, and build a transfer plan for each one.

---

detailSections.title: Audit Sales Dependency
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Who generates new leads?** - If the answer is primarily you (your network, your reputation, your LinkedIn), that is a dependency risk. Score 1-10 for how well the business generates leads without you.
- [ ] **Who closes deals?** - If customers buy because of their relationship with you personally, the sales process is not transferable. Score 1-10.
- [ ] **Is there a documented sales process?** - Could a competent salesperson follow your approach and get similar results? Or is it all instinct and personal rapport?
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win: Record your next 3 sales conversations (with permission). Transcribe the key parts. This is the fastest way to start documenting a sales process that currently lives in your head.

detailSections.title: Audit Operations Dependency
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **What operational decisions require your approval?** - List them. If more than 5 decisions per week need you personally, operations are too dependent.
- [ ] **Could your team deliver your core product or service without you for 30 days?** - If no, identify exactly which parts of delivery depend on your involvement.
- [ ] **Are your key processes documented?** - Check against your work in the Processes playbook. Undocumented processes are untransferable processes.
- [ ] **Do you have single points of failure in your team?** - Any role where one person leaving would cause serious disruption is a transferability risk, even if that person is not you.

detailSections.title: Audit Strategy Dependency
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Who sets the direction of the business?** - If every strategic decision requires your input, the business has no strategic independence. This is normal for small businesses but reduces transferability.
- [ ] **Is there a documented strategy or business plan?** - Even a simple one-page plan means someone else could understand where the business is heading and why.
- [ ] **Do your team members understand the "why" behind major decisions?** - If they only know the "what," they cannot make good decisions when you are not there.

detailSections.title: Audit Relationship Dependency
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List your 10 most important business relationships** - Key customers, suppliers, partners, referral sources, advisors.
- [ ] **How many of these relationships are with you personally rather than with the business?** - If you left, would these people stay?
- [ ] **For each at-risk relationship, identify a team member who could build a parallel relationship** - Start introducing them now. Relationship transfers take time.
- [ ] **Check your supplier agreements** - Are contracts in the business name or yours personally? Personal guarantees and informal handshake deals are transferability risks.

detailSections.title: Build Your Transfer Roadmap
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Rank your dependency risks from highest to lowest** - Which dependencies would cause the most damage if you were suddenly unavailable?
- [ ] **For your top 3 risks, define a specific transfer action** - Document the process, hire or train a replacement, introduce a team member to the relationship, or create a decision-making framework.
- [ ] **Set a 12-month timeline** - Transferability does not happen overnight. Plan to reduce your top 3 dependencies over the next year, one per quarter.
- [ ] **Test it** - Take a week away from the business. Not a holiday where you check email. A real test where the business runs without you. The gaps that appear are your remaining dependencies.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear picture of where the business depends on you personally
- Dependency scores across sales, operations, strategy, and relationships
- Top 3 dependency risks identified and prioritised
- A 12-month roadmap to reduce owner dependency
- A plan for testing transferability with real time away from the business

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This is one of the most uncomfortable actions in the entire Profit System. Most owners intellectually agree they should be less involved but emotionally resist actually stepping back. They enjoy being needed, or they believe nobody else can do it as well as they can.

The coaching opportunity is reframing dependency as a liability, not a strength. "The business needs me" sounds positive but actually means "the business is fragile." Help clients see that building transferability is building strength, not losing control.

Start with operations. It is the easiest area to transfer because processes can be documented and trained. Sales is hardest because it often involves the owner's personal reputation. Tackle that one last but start thinking about it early.
